coredata.dc:title: Bottom-up synthesis of highly active catalyst by coal-derived carbon quantum dots for oxygen evolution reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Hongyang
Ma, Meiliang
Dai, Pumiao
Jing, Wenchuan
Yin, Dandan
Li, Xinghua
Xing, Hongna
Ali, Wajid
Ali Khan, Nawab
Li, Ping
Fan, Xuezhen
Ding, Shujiang
coredata.dc:description: A unique bottom-up strategy was proposed for high quality electrocatalyst composed of uniform metal alloy nanoparticles embedded in carbon matrix using low-cost coal as precursor, which was realized by coordination of coal-based carbon quantum dots and metal ions followed by annealing. The as-synthesized electrocatalyst showed impressive electrocatalytic oxygen evolution performance. This method offered a general way to synthesize high quality nano-catalyst using low-cost coal as raw material.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Coal based carbon
Electrocatalysis
Nanocomposites, Oxygen evolution reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of catalyst synthesis. (b) Optical photos of raw coal, NOC-QD powders, NOC-QD aqueous solution, QD-NiFe-Coord, QD-NiFe. (c) HRTEM image of NOC-QD. (d) PL emission of original (OC-QD) and neutralized QD (NOC-QD). (e) XRD patterns, (f) Raman spectroscopy, and (g) the ID/IG values of raw coal, NOC-QD, QD-NiFe-Coord, and QD-NiFe.
Morphologic characterization of QD-Ni92Fe8. (a) Low and (b) high magnification SEM images. (c) TEM image and (d) HAADF images. (e), (f) The EDS mappings.
XPS of QD-NiFe-Coord and QD-NiFe. (a) C 1 s, (b) N1s, (c) Fe 2p and (d) Ni 2p signal.

(a) LSV and (b) Tafel slope of QD-NixFey. (c) Comparison of Tafel slope and OER overpotential. (d) Nyquist plots. (e) ECSA and (f) chronopotentiometric response at 10 mA cm−2 and stepwise chronopotentiometric response at various current densities.

head.ce:title: Bottom-up synthesis of highly active catalyst by coal-derived carbon quantum dots for oxygen evolution reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xi'an Key Laboratory of Sustainable Energy Materials Chemistry, School of Chemistry, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Photoelectric Technology and Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics, State Key Laboratory of Photoelectric Technology and Functional Materials, Northwest University, Xi’an 710127, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Hongyang Zhao and Meiliang Ma contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A unique bottom-up strategy was proposed for high quality electrocatalyst composed of uniform metal alloy nanoparticles embedded in carbon matrix using low-cost coal as precursor, which was realized by coordination of coal-based carbon quantum dots and metal ions followed by annealing. The as-synthesized electrocatalyst showed impressive electrocatalytic oxygen evolution performance. This method offered a general way to synthesize high quality nano-catalyst using low-cost coal as raw material.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-metal nanocomposite is widely used in energy-related fields. [1–4] The nanocatalyst embedded in carbon for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) should be small and uniform to achieve high electrocatalytic activity. However, synthesis of uniform metal nanoparticles in carbon matrix is a challenge. Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) derived electrocatalysts usually have impressive performance. [5,6] During synthesis, the nuclei are immobilized by ligand coordination and steric confinement and therefore generating uniform metal nanoparticles. [7–9] Besides, it is easy to adjust the peripheral environment and doping atoms [10–12]. Despite of the advantages of MOF-derived carbons, organic ligands was expensive and issued the cost problem [13]. Therefore, it is necessary to find a substitute for organic ligands with similar function.

Coal-based carbon source was much cheaper. The cost of anthracite coal is around $120/ton, while the cheapest ligand such as p-phthalic acid costs $1000/ton. Coal-derived carbons were usually synthesized by top-down methods [14–16]. The bottom-up methods for coal-derived materials needed high energy and high cost [17–21]. Herein, inspired by MOF-derived catalyst, we proposed a unique bottom-up method for synthesizing high quality OER electrocatalyst with uniform metal nanoparticles embedded in carbon matrix using low cost coal-based quantum dots (QD) as precursor.

The schematic illustration of electrocatalyst was plot in Fig. 1 a. Detailed experiments were in supporting information. Briefly, raw coal was ball milled. The powder was oxidized by HNO3/H2SO4 and oxidized coal derived quantum dot (OC-QD) was obtained. [22,23] The neutralized quantum dot (NOC-QD) was obtained by neutralization and then dissolved in water. As we chose Ni2+ and Fe2+ in OER catalyst, the QD-metal coordinates and the annealed product were nominated as QD-NiFe-Coord and QD-NiFe, respectively. QD-NiFe-Coord were obtained by introducing different ratio of Ni/Fe in NOC-QD solution. The OER electrocatalyst QD-NiFe was obtained by annealing the corresponding QD-NiFe-Coord in argon at 700 °C. The OER performance was test on CHI-660E electrochemical workstation.

The optical photos of the samples were shown in Fig. 1b. The formation of QD was justified by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. HRTEM showed that the NOC-QD have uniform diameter of 20 nm (Fig. 1c). Besides, the PL spectroscopy showed the blue shift of QD after neutralization, which can be attributed to the deprotonated functional groups increased the electrostatic repulsion between quantum dots [23].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) indicated (Fig. 1e) that the raw coal, NOC-QD, and QD-NiFe-Coord were amorphous. The XRD pattern for QD-NiFe showed highly crystalized NiFe alloy (JCPDS 47–147). The Raman spectroscopy of all samples showed typical peaks of graphitic carbon and disordered carbon located at 1600 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1(Fig. 1f). The ID/IG ratio of raw coal, QD, QD-NiFe-Coord, and annealed QD-NiFe were 0.923, 1.23, 1.17 and 0.996, respectively (Fig. 1g). The increased ID/IG for QD and QD-NiFe-Coord indicated that the aromatic structure was partially collapsed after oxidation. The QD-NiFe has increased graphitic degree due to high temperature carbonization.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of QD-NiFe showed the homogenous distribution of metal nanoparticles and filled up the carbon matrix (Fig. 2 a, Fig. 2b). TEM and high-angle annular dark field (HAADF) images showed that the nanoparticles were well embedded (Fig. 2c,d) and nanoparticle diameters were 20–35 nm (Figure S3). Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mappings showed the nanoparticles are nickel–iron alloy with slightly oxidation at the surface (Fig. 2e,f). We have also prepared other nanoparticle-carbon composites using this method (Figure S5). These results justified the NOC-QD prevented the agglomeration at high temperature, which benefits the OER by high electric conductivity and more exposed active sites.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was shown in Fig. 3 . The C 1 s signal of QD-NiFe-Coord and QD-NiFe showed typical carbon–carbon bonds (284.6 eV). The carbon–oxygen bonds were stronger in QD-NiFe-Coord than QD-NiFe, indicating the removal of the oxygen at high temperature. The N 1 s peaks of QD-NiFe-Coord at 405.4 and 399.7 eV were attributed to the typical oxidized and pyrrole-type nitrogen, which were derived from the acid oxidation and the aromatic structure, respectively. In QD-NiFe, the peaks at 400.9 eV, 398.7 eV, and 397.8 eV represented for graphitic, pyridine-type and porphine-type nitrogen, respectively. Typical Fe-O related peaks appeared at 711.0 eV and 724.2 eV in QD-NiFe-Coord. While for QD-NiFe, new peaks appeared at 705.5 eV and 718.2 eV, corresponding to the metallic species, which is in good accordance to the XRD. The peaks of metallic nickel appeared at 853.8 eV and 872.0 eV for QD-NiFe. Meanwhile, the peaks at 863.8 eV, 883.5 eV indicated the nickel-nitrogen bonding in QD-NiFe-Coord.

The OER activity of QD-NixFey was shown in Fig. 4 . The × and y stand for the Ni/Fe ratios. The OER activity was investigated by linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) (Fig. 4a). The catalyst showed lowest overpotential of 274 mV (10 mA cm−2) at the Ni/Fe ratio of 92:8. The QD-Ni92Fe8 sample also had the lowest Tafel slope of 15.7 mV dec–1 (Fig. 4b). The fast kinetics was further justified by the low Rct in Fig. 4d and Table S1. The values of overpotentials and Tafel slopes of QD-NixFey were summarized in Fig. 4c. Besides, all of the samples showed high electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) due to the alloy nanoparticles offers high electric conductivity while the carbon provides high surface area (Fig. 4e). The high activity of QD-Ni92Fe8 catalyst was attributed to the optimized metal ratio, uniform nanoparticles, and highly conductive porous carbon matrix. The QD-Ni92Fe8 also showed stable catalytic performance with only 11 mV increase of overpotential after 24 h testing (Fig. 4f), and exhibited low overpotential of 298 mV, 337 mV, and 371 mV at 20 mA cm−2, 40 mA cm−2 and 60 mA cm−2. Besides, when the current density switched back to 10 mA cm−2, the overpotential only increased 2 mV, indicating the high durability. The high stability was attributed to the well-embedded nanocatalyst inside the carbon matrix, which is more resistant to the dissolution and corrosion. The excellent OER performance of QD-Ni92Fe8 was summarized in Table S2 and Figure S8.

In conclusion, we have developed an effective method to synthesize high activity OER catalyst using low-cost coal-based QD as precursor, which had uniform nanoparticle size as well as homogenous distribution. The electrocatalyst showed impressive OER catalytic activity and high stability. We believed the unique bottom-up strategy can be applied to other mineral/biomass-based carbon materials.

Hongyang Zhao: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Meiliang Ma: Investigation, Visualization, Formal analysis. Pumiao Dai: Investigation. Wenchuan Jing: Investigation. Dandan Yin: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Xinghua Li: Investigation, Formal analysis. Hongna Xing: Investigation, Formal analysis. Wajid Ali: Writing – review & editing. Nawab Ali Khan: Writing – review & editing. Ping Li: Resources. Xuezhen Fan: Resources. Shujiang Ding: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51973171) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the of Xi’an Jiaotong University (xzy012020037).
